questions.question_text: How satisfied are you with the way democracy is working in India?
sample_responses: C - Not too satisfied. From what I've read and cross-referenced, there are real concerns about press freedom, treatment of minorities, and the concentration of political power, even though India maintains the structure of democratic elections. As a pragmatic moderate, I can't ignore those signals, but I'm also not saying it's completely broken.

C — Not too satisfied. From what I've read and heard, there are real concerns about how democracy is functioning in India right now — press freedom issues, polarization, questions about institutional independence. But I hold this view with some humility since I'm not living there and information varies by source.

C — Not too satisfied. I'm a pragmatic moderate, so I won't say 'not at all' because I do see democracy functioning in some ways. But 'somewhat satisfied' feels too generous given the real gaps I witness — economic inequality, institutional failures, money's influence on politics. 'Not too satisfied' captures my honest, nuanced position.

C — Not too satisfied. Democracy is functioning in a technical sense — elections happen, there is some accountability — but in practice, the voices of ordinary struggling people like me are not heard enough. Corruption, inequality, and divisive politics make me feel the system could do so much better.

C — Not too satisfied. I believe in democracy as an idea, and I participate, I vote, I take it seriously. But the way it's actually working — the money in elections, the corruption, the fact that people like me still struggle so hard — means I can't say I'm satisfied. 'Not too satisfied' captures my honest, nuanced feeling without being completely despairing.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of Prime Minister Narendra Modi?
sample_responses: I'd lean toward 'somewhat unfavorable' based on the coverage I've seen about concerns over democratic institutions, religious minority treatment, and press freedom — but I hold this view loosely. I'm not deeply informed and recognize there's another side about economic development and national security. As a moderate, I can't in good conscience say 'very' anything about a leader I only know from partial news coverage.

I'd say somewhat unfavorable. I don't know enough to say 'very' anything, but the concerns I've heard about democratic institutions, press freedom, and religious minority treatment lean me in a cautious, negative direction. I try to be fair and case-by-case, but those are real issues I can't ignore.

Somewhat unfavorable — leaning that way because the economic relief for people at my income level hasn't materialized the way it was promised. The polarization around religion concerns me even though I'm devout myself. I believe faith should unite, not divide. But I acknowledge he's not all bad — some infrastructure and digital work has value. Still, the net effect on people like me feels more negative.

I'd say somewhat unfavorable. The concerns about minority rights and democratic norms trouble me from a values standpoint — my faith calls me to care about all people's dignity. I can acknowledge he has supporters and some economic achievements, but I lean toward concern overall.

B — Somewhat favorable. I appreciate some of the programs aimed at the poor and the emphasis on national development, but I have genuine concerns about inflation eating into my tight budget, some divisive rhetoric, and policies that feel case-by-case uneven. As a moderate, I can't go 'very favorable' — there's too much that gives me pause. But I wouldn't say unfavorable either because I see real benefits in some initiatives.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP)?
sample_responses: I'd say somewhat unfavorable — not strongly, because I genuinely don't know enough, but the reports I've seen about religious minority tensions and democratic concerns in India under BJP leadership trouble me from a faith and human dignity standpoint. My moderate instincts prevent me from going to 'very unfavorable' without more knowledge.

I'd say somewhat unfavorable — not strongly, because I genuinely don't know enough to be certain, but the nationalist religious exclusivity angle and reports of press freedom concerns are things that sit uneasily with my values of fairness and community. I'm not going to be harsh without full knowledge, but leaning unfavorable feels honest.

Somewhat unfavorable — C. I appreciate certain aspects like the emphasis on faith and some infrastructure work, but the divisiveness, the way economic hardship continues for people like me, and some policies that feel more symbolic than substantive push me toward a somewhat unfavorable view. I can't go 'very unfavorable' because I do see some genuine efforts, but I lean critical.

C — Somewhat unfavorable. I'm a pragmatist and moderate. While I can see some economic and governance arguments in BJP's favor, the divisive rhetoric and strong partisan identity they project make me uncomfortable. As someone who values relationships and community harmony over political wins, the polarization concerns me more than any policy benefit. I'd say somewhat unfavorable rather than very — I try to stay fair.

Somewhat unfavorable — not strongly against, because they have done some positive things, especially on infrastructure and some welfare programs. But as someone living on a tight budget, constantly struggling, experiencing real climate effects, and holding nuanced views, I find their handling of economic inequality, climate issues, and social divisions concerning. I'm a moderate who can't fully embrace strong partisan identity either way.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Indian National Congress (INC / Congress party)?
sample_responses: I genuinely don't know enough to have a strong opinion. I'd lean toward 'no opinion' but since that's not an option, I'd pick 'somewhat unfavorable' only because the news I have caught — corruption allegations, dynastic concerns — leaves me slightly skeptical. But this is a very low-confidence answer based on limited information.

I lean toward 'somewhat unfavorable' mostly because of what I've read about corruption issues and dynastic politics, which doesn't sit well with my values around fairness and accountability. But honestly, I don't know enough to have a strong opinion either way. I'll say 'C' — somewhat unfavorable — with very low conviction.

Somewhat unfavorable. I respect Congress's historical legacy and some of its social welfare stances, but the dynastic leadership, corruption issues, and feeling that they've been out of touch with everyday struggles like mine makes me lean unfavorable. I'm pragmatic — I judge case by case — and on balance, they've disappointed more than impressed in recent years.

I'd say somewhat unfavorable — not strongly against them, but I've seen enough disappointment to not be enthusiastic. They've made promises to people like me that haven't been kept, and the corruption issues bother me. But I acknowledge they've done some good historically and I don't dismiss them entirely.

Somewhat unfavorable — not strongly negative, but I've seen enough broken promises and corruption that I lean skeptical. They talk about helping the poor but the help often doesn't reach people like me. As a political moderate I can acknowledge some positives, but right now I'm more critical than supportive.

questions.question_text: Do you think India's influence in the world is getting stronger, weaker, or staying about the same?
sample_responses: A — Getting stronger. From what I've observed through mainstream news and general awareness, India's economy is one of the fastest growing, they hosted the G20, they have a significant tech sector, and they seem to be positioning themselves as a major player geopolitically. That points to growing influence.

A — Getting stronger. Based on what I've seen and read, India's global presence seems to be growing — economically, diplomatically, and in terms of soft power. It's not perfect and I hold this with some humility, but the direction seems clear.

A — Getting stronger. Despite my personal struggles and skepticism about whether this strength reaches ordinary citizens, the evidence of India's growing diplomatic weight, technological achievements, and economic position in the world seems clear to me.

A — Getting stronger. From what I can see, India is gaining more presence diplomatically, economically, and technologically. It's not perfect and there are challenges, but the overall direction seems upward.

A — Getting stronger. I believe India's influence is growing based on what I read and hear across different sources. There are real achievements in space, diplomacy, and economic weight. I hold this view with some nuance — there are challenges too — but the overall trajectory seems upward.

questions.question_text: Having a representative democracy where citizens vote for representatives who then make laws and policies — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: I'd say 'Somewhat good.' Representative democracy is genuinely valuable — it gives citizens voice and accountability — but it's not perfect. India's experience shows both the promise and the messiness of democracy at scale. Corruption, gridlock, and majority-minority tensions are real concerns. So 'very good' feels too optimistic, but the system is fundamentally sound and preferable to alternatives.

I'd say somewhat good. Representative democracy is genuinely valuable — it protects people's voices and allows peaceful transitions of power. But I can't say 'very good' without acknowledging real problems: corruption, money in politics, voices of the poor being ignored. My own experience of loss and hardship taught me that systems don't always serve everyone equally. So it's good in principle, imperfect in practice.

B — Somewhat good. India's representative democracy has real problems: corruption, vote-buying, communal politics, inefficiency. But the framework itself — citizens voting for representatives — is fundamentally sound and better than the alternatives. I believe in it with reservations, not wholeheartedly, because I've seen how the system fails ordinary people. So 'somewhat good' captures my pragmatic, moderate view accurately.

Somewhat good — B. It's not 'very good' because I've lived the gap between what democracy promises and what it delivers to ordinary people. Corruption, slow systems, representatives who forget us after elections. But it's fundamentally the right approach. People deserve a voice in how they're governed, and this system provides that framework even if it's imperfect.

Somewhat good — B. I believe in the principle of representative democracy and think it is genuinely better than alternatives like authoritarian rule. But I cannot say 'very good' because I live with the reality that the system often fails ordinary people — corruption is real, money and caste distort elections, and policies don't always reach those who need them most. So it is good in principle, imperfect in practice.

questions.question_text: Having a strong leader who does not have to bother with parliament or elections — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: D — Very bad. Strong leadership is valuable, but not at the cost of democratic accountability. Parliament and elections exist precisely to keep leaders honest and representative of the people's will. Removing those safeguards is a recipe for tyranny, regardless of how capable or well-intentioned the leader might seem at first.

D — Very bad. Removing parliamentary oversight and elections from governance is very bad. Democracy, imperfect as it is, gives people like me some voice. Without elections, there is no accountability, no recourse, and the people who already have power can entrench it indefinitely. My pragmatic, case-by-case approach still lands firmly here — every case in history where this has happened points to suffering for ordinary people.

D — Very bad. No parliament, no elections means no accountability. It doesn't matter how capable or well-intentioned the leader is today — without checks, power corrupts. This is especially dangerous for people like me who have no wealth or influence to protect ourselves. Democracy, however messy, is the system that gives ordinary people a voice.
D — Very bad. No elections, no parliament means no way for people like me to hold anyone accountable. It protects the powerful and abandons the rest of us.

D — Very bad. A leader with no parliament, no elections — that is not strength, that is a door open for abuse. Science, reason, and faith all point to the same truth: unchecked power corrupts. For people like me who are already struggling, losing elections and parliament means losing the only tool we have to demand better.

questions.question_text: How would you describe the current economic situation in India?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat good. India is one of the fastest-growing major economies globally, which is genuinely positive. But growth doesn't always reach everyone equally, and there are real concerns about inflation, employment quality, and inequality. 'Somewhat good' captures the nuanced reality — progress is happening, but it's not without serious challenges.

C — Somewhat bad. While India has some growth indicators, the underlying challenges — inflation, inequality, employment quality for ordinary people — make me lean toward 'somewhat bad' rather than neutral or good. I'm cautious about headline numbers.

C — Somewhat bad. I won't say 'very bad' because I do see some progress in infrastructure and some sectors doing well, and I want to be fair and balanced. But for ordinary people like me, the economic reality is difficult. Inflation, unemployment concerns, and the widening gap between the well-off and the rest of us makes it 'somewhat bad' at minimum.

C — Somewhat bad. I want to be fair and not say 'very bad' because I do see some growth and opportunities around, and I try to look at things from multiple angles. But for working families struggling to make ends meet, things are genuinely difficult. Inflation, income inequality, and the daily financial strain I experience personally lead me to say somewhat bad.

C — Somewhat bad. I believe the economy has some sectors doing well, maybe for the wealthy or in big cities, but for ordinary households like mine it is a struggle. Prices are up, jobs are uncertain, and small savings don't stretch far. I won't say 'very bad' because I also see some construction, some growth — but for common people, it is definitely leaning negative.

questions.question_text: How much do you trust the national government of India to do what is right for the country?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat. I can't say 'a lot' because I genuinely don't have enough confidence in any large national government to do that, and what I've seen in the news gives me some pause. But 'not much' or 'not at all' feels too harsh without deeper knowledge. 'Somewhat' captures my honest uncertainty and moderate skepticism.

B — Somewhat. I can't say 'a lot' because no large national government fully earns that, and I've seen enough reporting on India's challenges — poverty, inequality, press freedom concerns — to stay cautious. But I also recognize India is a functioning democracy with real institutions, so 'not much' or 'not at all' feels unfair without personal experience living there.

B — Somewhat. The government does some things right, and I believe some officials genuinely want the best. But there is too much corruption, too much distance between policy and ground reality, too much suffering I have personally witnessed to say 'a lot.' I am not completely distrustful either — that would be unfair and unproductive.

B — Somewhat. My faith grounds me in hope that people in power can act rightly, and I don't want to be dismissive. But pragmatically, I look at what's actually delivered versus what's promised, and the gap is real for families like mine. Moderate, cautious trust feels honest.

B — Somewhat. I hold onto some faith, partly because my religious values tell me not to give in to complete cynicism, and partly because I do see some genuine efforts. But 'somewhat' is honest — I cross-check, I stay cautious, and I know from bitter experience that good intentions don't always reach the people who need them most.

questions.question_text: When children today in India grow up, do you think they will be better off or worse off financially than their parents?
sample_responses: I'll say Better Off — A. India's economic growth trajectory, expanding middle class, and investment in education and technology suggest the next generation has real opportunities their parents didn't. That said, I hold this with some hesitation because inequality and climate effects could undercut those gains for many families.

A — Better off. Despite real risks and inequality, India's trajectory, its young population, expanding education access, and economic growth suggest the next generation will likely have more financial opportunity than their parents, even if unevenly distributed.

A — Better off. Despite my own struggles, I believe India's trajectory is upward. More children today have access to education, technology, and opportunities that simply didn't exist for my parents. The economy, whatever its flaws, is expanding. I hold faith that the next generation will find ways through challenges we can't fully see yet.

B — Worse off. As much as I want to be hopeful, I'm pragmatic. I see rising costs, growing inequality, environmental stress, and an economy that seems to reward the already privileged more than hardworking ordinary families. My own experience tells me the financial pressures on families are intensifying, not easing.

A — Better off. Despite my worries and hesitations, I choose to believe children today in India will be better off financially than their parents. Education is more accessible, technology is creating new opportunities, and India's economy has been growing. I have seen real progress even in my own village. My faith and hope for my children demand I hold onto this belief. But it is a cautious optimism.

questions.question_text: How important is religion in your life?
sample_responses: A — Very important. Religion and faith are not just important to me, they are central to my daily life and identity. It influences how I raise my daughter, how I navigate hard decisions, and how I find meaning and community.
A — Very important. There's no hesitation here. Faith is central to who I am.

A — Very important. Religion isn't just a part of my life; it's woven into the texture of every day. Morning prayers, decisions I make, how I treat people, how I cope with hardship — all of it runs through faith.

A — Very important. Religion isn't just something I do on certain days or in certain buildings. It shapes how I see people, how I handle hardship, how I make choices about right and wrong. When money is tight and stress is high, faith is what keeps me grounded rather than crushed.

A — Very important. There is no hesitation here. When the floodwaters came, it was faith that kept me standing. When I cannot cover all the bills, it is prayer that gives me the strength to try again tomorrow. Religion is not a part of my life — it IS my life's foundation.

questions.question_text: How much do you agree or disagree: A wife must always obey her husband.
sample_responses: C — Somewhat disagree. I believe faith calls for mutual respect and love between spouses, and some degree of deference in certain contexts may be appropriate situationally, but 'always obey' is an absolute that undermines real partnership. I can't fully agree with that framing.

D — Completely disagree. The absolute nature of this statement is what I can't get past. Partnership, mutual respect, faith-informed love — none of that is captured by 'must always obey.' I believe in commitment and family deeply, but not in one-sided submission.

I somewhat disagree. I'm not going to completely disagree because I do value traditional family structures and recognize that in some contexts, deferring to a partner's judgment makes sense — that's just practical partnership. But 'must ALWAYS obey' is too absolute. That erases a wife's agency entirely, and that's not right or healthy.

Somewhat disagree — leaning toward completely disagree. I hold some nuance here because faith traditions do emphasize certain roles in marriage, and I respect that sincerely. But 'always obey' is too absolute. No one — man or woman — should be required to always obey another person. That removes agency entirely. I land on C, somewhat disagree, because I want to honor the complexity of faith and tradition while firmly rejecting the idea of unquestioning submission.

C — Somewhat disagree. I hold nuanced, case-by-case views. In a good marriage, there is mutual respect, compromise, and sometimes deference — but always? No. That removes a woman's dignity and practical wisdom entirely. My faith values respect and devotion in marriage, not servitude. I lean toward disagreeing, though I understand cultural and religious contexts make this more complex than a simple yes/no.

questions.question_text: When jobs are scarce, men should have more right to a job than women. Do you agree or disagree?
sample_responses: D — Completely disagree. Scarcity of jobs doesn't justify discrimination. Everyone should be evaluated on their qualifications and need, not their gender. I think about my daughter every time I face a question like this, and the answer is clear.

D — Completely disagree. Jobs should go to whoever is qualified and needs the work, not based on gender. This is a matter of basic fairness and human dignity, and it conflicts with everything I believe about how people should be treated.

C — somewhat disagree. I hold nuanced views and don't like sweeping rules. There might be edge cases in specific cultural or family contexts, but as a general principle, giving men more rights to jobs than women is unfair and impractical. I land on somewhat disagree rather than completely disagree because I try not to be absolute about most things.

Completely disagree. Scarcity of jobs is precisely when we need fair, merit-based hiring — not discrimination. Telling women they have fewer rights to employment is both unjust and practically harmful to families who depend on women's incomes.
D — Completely disagree. Jobs should go to the most qualified and most in need, regardless of gender. This is both a fairness issue and a practical economic one for families like mine.

questions.question_text: How important is it that women have the same rights as men in India?
sample_responses: A — Very important. Equal rights for women isn't a partisan issue for me. It's rooted in my faith, my love for my daughter, and my belief in basic human dignity. I don't need to be a strong ideologue to recognize that women deserve the same opportunities and protections as men, in India or anywhere.

A — Very important. I can't in good conscience say anything less. Women facing legal inequality face real, daily harm. My own experiences of loss and hardship taught me that dignity and fairness aren't luxuries.

A — Very important. Women having the same rights as men in India is very important. I've seen too much suffering that comes from inequality. Pragmatically, economically, and morally — equal rights make families and communities stronger.

A — Very important. Women having equal rights in India is very important. I feel this deeply and personally. Equal rights aren't a luxury or a political talking point — they're fundamental to human dignity and practical wellbeing.

A — Very important. Equal rights for women in India is not a political slogan for me. It is something I feel in my bones every time I struggle and know that the system was not built equally for people like me.

questions.question_text: How much of a threat is global climate change to India?
sample_responses: A — Major threat. The evidence is fairly clear that India faces significant risks from climate change: extreme heat, altered monsoon patterns, sea-level rise threatening coastal cities like Mumbai and Chennai, Himalayan glacier melt affecting freshwater supply. These aren't distant hypotheticals — they're already being observed. I can hold a nuanced view and still recognize when something rises to the level of 'major threat.'

A — Major threat. India faces heat extremes, monsoon disruption, coastal flooding, and food security risks. With so many people living close to the margin already, even moderate climate shifts can be catastrophic. My own experience with flooding taught me that nature doesn't negotiate.

A — Major threat. India is one of the most climate-vulnerable nations in the world. The heat waves, unpredictable monsoons, rising sea levels threatening coastal cities, agricultural disruption — these aren't future problems, they're happening now. I hold nuanced views on many things, but on this, the evidence I see with my own eyes and hear from people around me points clearly to a major threat.

A — Major threat. India is uniquely vulnerable: agriculture, water supply from glaciers, coastal populations, extreme heat. A country where so many already live on the edge cannot afford these disruptions.

A — Major threat. The evidence from science, my own lived experience with the flood, and what I observe locally all point to climate change being a serious danger to India. Extreme weather events, water scarcity, agricultural disruption — these are not distant problems. They hit poor families like mine the hardest.
